title: The right, and what prior approval is not
paras: Let's start with the right itself — and with what prior approval is not. Class M-A of Part 3 of Schedule 2 to the G-P-D-O 2015 grants planning permission for the change of use of a building, and land within its curtilage, from Class E — commercial, business and service — to Class C3, dwellinghouses. It's the principal route by which shops, offices, restaurants, gyms, clinics, nurseries and light industrial premises become housing without a planning application. It was inserted by S-I 2021 number 428, with effect from the 1st of August 2021. It replaced the former Class O — office to residential — and absorbed most of the piecemeal commercial-to-residential rights, following the consolidation of the old A1, A2, A3, B1 and various D1 and D2 uses into Class E in September 2020.

The structure is the whole point. The permission is granted by Article 3(1) of the Order itself — permitted development rights are grants of planning permission, that's Keenan — and what the developer obtains from the authority is not permission, but prior approval of a defined and closed list of matters, set out in paragraph M-A 2(2). The principle of the change of use is settled by the Order. The authority can't refuse a valid scheme because residential use is unacceptable in principle, because employment floorspace should be retained, or because the development conflicts with the development plan.

The development plan is largely displaced. Prior approval isn't determined under section 38(6). The authority must have regard to the N-P-P-F, but only so far as it's relevant to the subject matter of the prior approval — that's paragraph W(10)(b). Plan evidence can inform judgment on the listed matters — noise policy, flood risk material, the identification of industrial areas — but plan policy hostile to conversion cannot found refusal on principle. An authority wishing to give such policy effect has one lawful route: an Article 4 direction. There's no policy basis for affordable housing through prior approval, and conditions can't go beyond the listed matters. The Community Infrastructure Levy can still be payable, though the in-use buildings deduction often reduces or extinguishes it.

The mechanics differ from an application too. There's a 56-day determination period, with a deemed consent if the authority is silent, and a fee charged per dwellinghouse — currently 260 pounds, materially below the application fee for the same units, though fees change regularly and should be checked before submission. Resist describing prior approval as a formality. It's a genuine consenting process. Applications fail regularly — most often on natural light, noise and transport — and a scheme that doesn't comply with every limitation and condition of the Class gains nothing from the process at all.

title: Qualifying buildings: the gateway tests
paras: Now, qualifying buildings — the gateway tests. The gateway is paragraph M-A 1(1)(b): the building must have been in a use within Class E for a continuous period of at least two years, ending with the date of the prior approval application. Three interpretive points decide cases. First, the period attaches to the application date, not the change of use. Second, for any part of the two years falling before the 1st of September 2020, the use must have fallen within the listed predecessor classes — A1, A2, A3, B1, D1(a), D1(b) or D2(e), other than indoor swimming pools and skating rinks — so a use within Class E today, but outside those classes then, may fail the test. And third, the use must be lawful. A use in breach of planning control can't found the qualifying period unless time has made it lawful — in which case a certificate of lawfulness is the sensible precursor.

The 2024 amendments — S-I 2024 number 141, from the 5th of March 2024 — removed the three-month vacancy requirement and the fifteen hundred square metre floorspace cap. Occupied buildings of any scale, including large office blocks, can now proceed. But vacancy still matters, in a different way. A vacant building generally retains its last lawful use, and short vacant periods won't normally break continuity — while a long-vacant building raises the question whether the Class E use subsisted or had been abandoned. That's a question of fact and degree, and it's exactly this stock that authorities scrutinise. Older commentary and appeal decisions discussing the vacancy rule or the cap describe the 2021 to 2024 law, and are of historic interest only.

The exclusions in M-A 1(1)(d) to (e) are absolute — no discretion, no balancing. Development is not permitted where the land is or forms part of an S-S-S-I, a listed building or land within its curtilage, a scheduled monument or its curtilage, a safety hazard area, or a military explosives storage area. Nor where the building is within a National Landscape — the statutory wording still says A-O-N-B — a National Park, the Broads, a World Heritage Site or a section 41(3) area. The listed building exclusion reaches unlisted buildings within a listed building's curtilage, and curtilage structures treated as part of the listing. Conservation areas are deliberately absent from the list. Class M-A applies in them, subject to the additional ground-floor matter — a distinction from many other permitted development rights that both sides routinely misunderstand. And agricultural tenancies need the documented consent of both landlord and tenant.

The development is the change of use of 'a building and any land within its curtilage'. Partial conversion is possible — ground-floor commercial with residential above is common — with the qualifying test and exclusions applied to the building, and the prior approval matters assessed against the development proposed. The end use must be C3 and nothing else. H-M-Os — that's C4 or sui generis — serviced apartments and short-term letting all fall outside the right, and paragraph M-A 2(6) requires the building to remain in C3 use thereafter. Sui generis commercial uses — the hot food takeaway, the betting office, the pub, the launderette — are not Class E, and can't use Class M-A at all, though some retain conversion rights under other Classes.

title: The 56 days, deemed consent and implementation
paras: On to the 56 days, deemed consent and implementation. Before beginning development, the developer applies for a determination as to whether prior approval is required as to the M-A 2(2) matters — procedurally under paragraph W, applied by M-A 2(4) with the transport modification. There are three outcomes: prior approval not required; required and given; or required and refused — and refusal on any one matter defeats the application. Paragraph W(2) prescribes the content — description, plan, contact details, fee — but in practice, floor plans identifying the number of dwellings, the layout, and window positions and dimensions are essential, because natural light can't be assessed without them. Paragraph W(3) lets the authority refuse where the development doesn't comply with the Class, or where insufficient information is provided — a refusal of prior approval carrying a right of appeal, and the proper route where the authority considers the scheme outside Class M-A altogether. Consultation follows paragraphs W(4) and W(5): the highway authority on transport, the Environment Agency in flood cases, and neighbour notification as prescribed.

Development must not begin before written notice of the determination, or the expiry of 56 days from receipt — whichever is earlier. That's the deemed consent. Three qualifications carry all the weight. The clock runs from receipt of a valid application, and disputes about when it started — fees, electronic submission — are common. The period can be extended, but only by written agreement: Gluck, reported at 2020, E-W-C-A Civ 1756, confirmed that Article 7 permits extension, and that an exchange of emails suffices — proposal and assent, construed objectively — displacing Warren Farm. And deemed consent adds nothing if the development doesn't qualify. Keenan holds that the authority's silence can't convert non-qualifying development into permitted development — so proceeding after 56 days on a scheme with unresolved gateway doubts is the developer's risk entirely.

Determination is confined to the listed matters, having regard to representations, and to the N-P-P-F so far as relevant — that's W(10). Conditions may be imposed only where reasonably related to the subject matter of the prior approval — W(13) — so general development management conditions are unlawful. Refusal, or non-determination where an extension removed the deemed consent, carries a section 78 appeal, usually by written representations, confined to the same matters. And where certainty matters — funders, purchasers, marginal qualification — only a section 192 lawful development certificate carries a conclusive presumption of lawfulness. Neither a grant of prior approval nor deemed consent does.

Two conditions attach to the development itself, and both bite harder than their planning-permission cousins. The development must be completed — not commenced — within three years of the prior approval date; that's the date approval is given, or the 56th day where no determination was notified. A stalled conversion that misses the deadline loses the right — that's M-A 2(5). And the development must be carried out in accordance with the details approved — or, on deemed consent, the details in the application — under W(12)(a). There's no section 73 or minor-material-amendment route for prior approvals. A post-approval design change needs a fresh prior approval, or a planning application for the changed element, secured before it's built.

title: The prior approval matters — and what is not one
paras: Now the prior approval matters — and what is not one. The matters are exhaustive — M-A 2(2) — and for each, the authority assesses the acceptability of the development by reference to that matter alone. Transport: the impacts of the development, particularly to ensure safe site access — safe vehicular and pedestrian access, servicing and refuse, intensification effects. Parking stress counts where it bears on safety or the operation of the network, but local plan parking standards can't be applied as if this were an application, and the trip comparison against the lawful commercial use frequently shows a net reduction. Contamination: the safety of future occupiers — a phase 1 desk study at minimum, with dry cleaners, garages, laboratories and light industrial histories warranting more. Flooding: risk in relation to the building — the Environment Agency consulted, and a site-specific flood risk assessment in zones 2 and 3. The sequential test doesn't govern prior approval, but the substance of national flood policy enters through W(10)(b), and ground-floor residential in high-risk zones is the recurring battleground.

Noise from commercial premises on the intended occupiers is one of the two most common refusals — the agent-of-change problem in reverse, protecting incoming residents and, by extension, the existing businesses from the complaints those residents would otherwise generate. It's noise from commercial premises, not traffic noise generally. Expect a qualified acoustician, survey data covering representative operating periods — including night time where neighbours trade late — and mitigation through glazing and ventilation that doesn't depend on sealed windows, unless alternative ventilation is adequate. In conservation areas, ground-floor changes attract the additional matter: the impact of the loss of ground-floor use on the character or sustainability of the area — the deliberate counterweight to conservation areas not being excluded. Its scope is the change of use, not external appearance, and the applicant's usual answer is vacancy history, marketing evidence, and the surrounding pattern of uses.

Natural light in all habitable rooms is the single most common reason for refusal. Introduced in 2020, after the criticism of poor office conversions, it requires the authority to be satisfied that every habitable room — living rooms, dining rooms, kitchens used as living space, bedrooms — receives adequate natural light. 'Adequate' is undefined. It's planning judgment, commonly informed by daylight modelling against recognised guidance, with rooms lit solely from lightwells, deep single-aspect plans and north-facing rooms attracting the closest scrutiny. Without floor plans showing window positions and dimensions, the authority is entitled to refuse. The industrial areas matter protects the living conditions of intended occupiers — noise, dust, odour, H-G-V movements, hours — where residential use is introduced into an area important for industry, waste or distribution. It is not a power to protect employment land, and an authority refusing to protect its employment stock under this heading errs in law. And the one loss-of-use matter is tightly confined: registered nurseries and N-H-S health centres, answered with evidence of alternative provision, spare capacity, or cessation regardless of the development.

What the authority cannot consider decides as many appeals as what it can. These are not prior approval matters: the principle of losing commercial or employment floorspace, outside the two protected categories; the adequacy of internal space beyond the Article 3(9A) floor; amenity space, refuse storage or play space; design and external appearance, absent operational works; affordable housing; and conformity with the development plan. Representations on such matters must be received and considered — and cannot found refusal.

title: The case law: what prior approval cannot do
paras: Now the case law — what prior approval cannot do. Keenan against Woking Borough Council, reported at 2017, E-W-C-A Civ 438, is the foundation: prior approval cannot enlarge the Class. The council's failure to respond in time to a Part 6 application did not give permission to a track that never qualified — neither a positive determination nor the expiry of the period can confer permission on development outside the Class. For Class M-A, the implications are fundamental. Deemed consent after 56 days is worthless if the building fails the two-year test, sits in an excluded designation, or otherwise breaches M-A 1 — and a granted prior approval is equally incapable of curing the defect. An authority's own error in validating and approving a non-qualifying scheme creates no permission, and a completed conversion resting on a defective claim is enforceable, notwithstanding the paperwork. Murrell, reported at 2010, E-W-C-A Civ 1367, holds the converse: where the development does qualify, the permission crystallises when the period expires, and the authority cannot resile.

New World Payphones against Westminster City Council — Court of Appeal, 2019 — applies Keenan to composition. The whole of a development must fall within the scope of the Class: description, exceptions, limitations and conditions. A mixed or dual-purpose development with a component outside the Class is not permitted development, and the compliant core can't be severed from the non-compliant whole after the event. For Class M-A, that bears on schemes bundling the change of use with what the Class doesn't permit: operational development, uses outside C3, land beyond the curtilage. Structure the scheme correctly at the outset, with separate planning applications for the elements Class M-A doesn't cover.

Dharmeshkumar, reported at 2025, E-W-H-C 2513, Admin, is the clearest modern statement that a prior approval is not a licence for the general concept of conversion. An office block converted to flats departed from the application drawings, and works forming part of the redevelopment were unlawful. The change of use lost the benefit of the right on two independent grounds. First, Article 3(5), under which unlawful operations associated with the development deprive it of the Order's protection. And second, W(12)(a), which itself ties the development to the details approved — with the application drawings consultable where the notice is ambiguous. So build what was approved. Keep the enabling works lawful. And where the scheme changes after approval, secure a fresh prior approval, or a planning application for the changed element, before building it — because no section 73 route exists.

Around the decided cases sits settled orthodoxy — reliable, but to be re-verified before formal citation. The scope of a prior approval matter is statutory construction, for the courts; its application to the facts is planning judgment, reviewable only on public law grounds. The matters are read purposively, not artificially narrowly. Refusal on unlisted grounds is unlawful. The adequacy of natural light is judgment on the evidence, and refusal is proper where the material doesn't demonstrate adequacy for every habitable room. Continuity of use, abandonment during vacancy, and the extent of curtilage are all fact and degree. And independently of the matters, Article 3(9A) disapplies the permission for any dwelling under 37 square metres gross internal area, or failing the nationally described space standard — schemes have failed on unit sizes even where the light and noise cases were sound.

title: Article 4, appraisal order and transactions
paras: Finally, Article 4, the appraisal order, and transactions. An Article 4(1) direction removes Class M-A from a defined area, restoring full development management control — including the development plan. It doesn't prohibit conversion, and it creates no presumption against it. It requires an application. Because the industrial areas matter protects occupiers rather than employment land, and the loss-of-use matter is confined to nurseries and health centres, the Article 4 direction is the only lawful means by which an authority can protect commercial floorspace from Class M-A — which is why directions are widespread in London, and in centres with strong office markets.

National policy disciplines the tool. Directions removing residential-conversion rights should be limited to situations where necessary to avoid wholly unacceptable adverse impacts, based on robust evidence, and applied to the smallest geographical area possible — and the Secretary of State has power to modify or cancel directions, and has used it against over-extensive ones. The compensation regime — sections 107 and 108 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 — is the other discipline. Withdraw the right and refuse what it would have permitted, and compensation for abortive expenditure and depreciation can follow — unless sufficient advance notice was given. That's why most Class M-A directions are non-immediate, with at least twelve months' notice. And that notice period is also the developer's window. A valid prior approval application made before the direction takes effect is determined under the right as it stood, and a prior approval already secured is not removed by a later direction — though delay with a direction pending is unwise.

The appraisal order for any Class M-A instruction runs like this. Article 4 directions, confirmed and proposed, reading the mapped boundary and the description of development withdrawn. The absolute exclusions, including curtilage listing. The two-year lawful Class E use, with the September 2020 lookback. Space standards, against the proposed layout. Then the prior approval matters, in order of risk — natural light first, noise second. The recurring failures mirror that order: daylight evidence absent or inadequate; qualifying-use misunderstandings; vacancy-and-abandonment confusion; units below the space standard; curtilage-listed structures missed at appraisal; noise surveys that missed the operating hours; imprecision about which parts of a mixed building change use; and implementation failures — off-drawing builds, unlawful works, the missed completion deadline.

On transactions, due diligence on a converted or converting building establishes four things — with evidence, rather than paperwork. First, that the building qualified at the application date. Second, that the development was built in accordance with the approved details. Third, that completion occurred within the three-year window. And fourth, that any external works had their own permission. Where any is doubtful, the lawful development certificate is the remedy — the prior approval documents alone prove none of them. On acquisitions for conversion, check proposed as well as confirmed directions, and price the twelve-month notice window into the programme.
